event: Reindeer hunters of the Hamburg and Bromme cultures settle southern Scandinavia as glaciers retreat at the end of the last Ice Age.
Farming arrives with the Funnelbeaker culture, which builds large megalithic dolmens and passage graves across the land.
The Nordic Bronze Age begins, leaving rich burials and artifacts such as the gilded Trundholm Sun Chariot.
The Pre-Roman Iron Age develops as Celtic and later Roman trade influences reach Jutland and the Danish isles.
Germanic tribes including the Jutes, Angles, and early Danes inhabit the region during the Roman Iron Age.
Jutes and Angles from the Danish peninsula join the migrations to Britain during the Migration Period.
Viking raiders, including Danes, attack the monastery at Lindisfarne, marking a symbolic start of the Viking Age.
King Godfred fortifies the Danevirke earthworks and the trading town of Hedeby on the southern border with the Franks.
The Great Heathen Army of Danish Vikings invades and conquers large parts of Anglo-Saxon England.
Harald Bluetooth becomes king and unites much of Denmark, raising the Jelling runestones.
Harald Bluetooth converts to Christianity, beginning the Christianization of the Danish kingdom.
Sweyn Forkbeard conquers England, briefly making the Danish king ruler of both realms.
Cnut the Great unites Denmark, England, and Norway into a powerful North Sea Empire.
King Canute IV is murdered in Odense and later canonized as Denmark's first royal saint.
Bishop Absalon founds a fortress at Copenhagen, which grows into the future capital.
According to legend the Dannebrog, the Danish flag, falls from the sky during the Battle of Lyndanisse in Estonia.
Queen Margaret I forges the Kalmar Union, joining Denmark, Norway, and Sweden under a single monarch.
The University of Copenhagen is founded, becoming Denmark's oldest institution of higher learning.
Sweden breaks away from the Kalmar Union under Gustav Vasa, leaving Denmark united with Norway.
King Christian III imposes the Lutheran Reformation, making Denmark a Protestant state.
Denmark cedes territory to Sweden by the Peace of Brömsebro after the Torstenson War.
The Treaty of Roskilde forces Denmark to surrender Scania and other eastern provinces to Sweden.
Frederick III establishes absolute hereditary monarchy after the disastrous wars with Sweden.
The Lex Regia codifies royal absolutism, granting the Danish king near-unlimited power.
Denmark fights Sweden in the Great Northern War as part of a coalition seeking to curb Swedish power.
Adscription (stavnsbånd) tying peasants to their estates is abolished in sweeping agrarian reforms.
The British fleet attacks Copenhagen in the first Battle of Copenhagen during the Napoleonic Wars.
Britain bombards Copenhagen and seizes the Danish fleet, pushing Denmark into alliance with Napoleon.
Denmark cedes Norway to Sweden under the Treaty of Kiel after backing the losing side in the Napoleonic Wars.
King Frederick VII signs the June Constitution, ending absolutism and establishing constitutional monarchy.
Denmark loses the Second Schleswig War to Prussia and Austria, ceding Schleswig, Holstein, and Lauenburg.
The Change of System establishes parliamentary government, requiring cabinets to have parliamentary support.
A constitutional reform grants women and servants the right to vote in national elections.
Denmark sells the Danish West Indies, now the U.S. Virgin Islands, to the United States.
Northern Schleswig is reunited with Denmark after a plebiscite following World War I.
Thorvald Stauning forms the first Social Democratic government, beginning the party's long dominance.
Nazi Germany invades and occupies Denmark on 9 April during World War II.
The Danish resistance helps ferry most of the country's Jews to safety in neutral Sweden.
Iceland declares full independence, ending its personal union with the Danish crown.
Denmark is liberated as German forces surrender and the country becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Denmark joins NATO as a founding member, ending its tradition of neutrality.
A new constitution introduces a single-chamber parliament and allows female succession to the throne.
Denmark joins the European Economic Community, the forerunner of the European Union.
Greenland gains home rule, beginning its move toward greater autonomy within the Danish realm.
Denmark becomes the first country in the world to legally recognize same-sex registered partnerships.
Danish voters reject the Maastricht Treaty, leading to the Edinburgh Agreement opt-outs.
The Øresund Bridge opens, linking Copenhagen to Sweden, while voters reject adopting the euro.
Copenhagen hosts the UN COP15 climate change summit.
Denmark legalizes same-sex marriage, allowing weddings within the state church.
Danes vote to abolish the EU defense opt-out following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
